verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And from Miletus he sent to Ephesus, and called the elders of the Church.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when they were come to him, he said unto them, Ye know, from the first day that I came into Asia, after what manner I have been with you at all seasons, serving the Lord with all humility of mind, and with many tears, and temptations, which befell me by the lying in wait of the Jews: and how I kept back nothing that was profitable unto you, but have showed you, and have taught you publicly, and from house to house, testifying both to the Jews, and also to the Greeks, repentance toward God and faith toward our Lord Jesus Christ.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See him, hasting to sail by, and yet not overlooking them, but taking order for all.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having sent for the rulers, through those he discourses to them (the Ephesians): but it is worthy of admiration, how finding himself under a necessity of saying certain great things about himself, he tries to make the least he can of it (πειρἅτα μετριάζειν).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For just as Samuel, when about to deliver up the government to Saul says in their presence, “Have I taken aught of your hands?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye are witnesses, and God also” (1 Sam. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 3, 5); (so Paul here).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: David also, when disbelieved, says, “I was with the flock keeping my father’s sheep: and when the bear came, I scared her away with my hands” (1 Sam. xvii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 34, 35): and Paul himself too says to the Corinthians, “I am become a fool; ye have compelled me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Cor. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, God Himself also does the same, not speaking of himself upon any and every occasion, but only when He is disbelieved, then He brings up His benefits.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Accordingly, see what Paul does here: first he adduces their own testimony: that you may not imagine his words to be mere boasting, he calls the hearers themselves as witnesses of the things he says, since he was not likely to speak lies in their presence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the excellence of a teacher, to have for witnesses of his merits those who are his disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what is wonderful, Not for one day nor for two, says he, have I continued doing this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He wishes to cheer them for the future, that they may bravely bear all things, both the parting from him, and the trials about to take place—just as it was in the case of Moses and Joshua.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And see how he begins: “How I have been with you the whole time, serving the Lord with all humility of mind.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, what most becomes rulers: “hating pride” (Exod. xviii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 21, LXX.), says (Moses): which (qualification) is especially in point for rulers, because to them there is (almost) a necessity of becoming arrogant.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This (humility) is the groundwork of all that is good, as in fact Christ saith, “Blessed are the poor in spirit.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And (here) not simply, “with humility of mind,” but, “with all humility.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For there are many kinds of humility, in word and in action, towards rulers, and toward the ruled.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Will you that I mention to you some kinds of humility?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: There are some who are lowly towards those who are lowly, and high towards the high: this is not the character of humility.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Some then are such.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then, that he may not seem to be arrogant, he lays a foundation beforehand, removing that suspicion: For, “if, says he, I have acted ‘with all humility of mind,’ it is not from arrogance that I say the things I say.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then for his gentleness, ever with much condescension making them his fellows.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With you,” he says, “have I been, serving the Lord;” he makes the good works common to them with himself: none of it his own peculiar.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What?” (you will say) “why, against God could he possibly bear himself arrogantly?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet there are many who do bear themselves arrogantly against God: but this man not even against his own disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is the merit of a teacher, by his own achievements of virtue to form the character of his disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then for his fortitude, upon which also he is very concise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With many tears,” he says, “and temptations which befell me by the lying in wait of the Jews.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you see that he grieves at their doings?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But here too he seems to show how sympathizing he was: for he suffered for those who were going to perdition, for the doers themselves: what was done to himself, he even rejoiced at it; for he belonged to that band which “rejoiced that they were counted worthy to suffer shame for that Name” (Acts v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 41): and again he says, “Now I rejoice in my sufferings for you” (Col. i.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24): and again, “For our light affliction, which is but for the moment, worketh for us a far more exceeding and eternal weight of glory.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: These things, however, he says, by way of making the least of his merits (μετριάζων).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But there he show his fortitude, not so much of daring, as of enduring: “I,” says he, “have been evil entreated, but it was with you: and what is indeed the grievous part of the business, at the hands of Jews.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe, he puts here both love and fortitude.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Mark, here, I pray you, a character of teaching: “I kept back nothing,” he says, ungrudging fulness, unshrinking promptness—“of what was profitable unto you:” because there were things which they did not need to learn.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For as the hiding some things would have been like grudging, so the saying all things would be folly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why he adds, “that was profitable unto you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But have showed you, and have taught you:” have not only said, but also taught: not doing this either as a mere matter of form.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For that this is what he means, observe what he says: “publicly, and from house to house:” thereby representing the exceeding toil, the great earnestness and endurance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Both Jews, and Greeks.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not (addressing myself) to you alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Testifying:” here, the boldness of speech: and that, even though we do no good, yet we must speak: for this is the meaning of “testifying,” when we speak to those who do not pay attention: and so the word διαμαρτύρασθαι is for the most part used.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I call heaven and earth to witness” (Deut. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26), διαμαρτύρομαι, Moses says: and now Paul himself, Διαμαρτυρόμενος “both to Jews and Greeks repentance toward God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What testifiest thou?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That they should be careful about their manner of life: that they should repent, and draw near to God.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Both to Jews and Greeks”—for neither did the Jews know Him—both by reason of their works, he says, “repentance towards God,” and, by reason that they knew not the Son, he adds, “and faith in the Lord Jesus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: To what end, then, sayest thou these things? to what end dost thou put them in mind of them?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What has come of it? hast thou anything to lay to their charge?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having first alarmed their feeling, then he adds, “And now, behold, I go bound in the Spirit unto Jerusalem, not knowing the things that shall befall me there: save that the Holy Ghost witnesseth in every city, saying that bonds and afflictions abide me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But none of these things move me, neither count I my life dear unto myself, so that I might finish my course with joy, and the ministry, which I have received of the Lord Jesus, to testify the Gospel of the grace of God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore says he this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By way of preparing them to be always ready to meet dangers, whether seen or unseen, and in all things to obey the Spirit.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that it is for great objects that he is led away from them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Save that the Holy Ghost,” he says, “in every city witnesseth to me saying”—to show that he departs willingly; that (see Hom. xlv. p.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 273) you may not imagine it any bond or necessity, when he says, “bound in the Spirit—that in every city bonds and afflictions await me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then also he adds this, “I count not my life dear, until I shall have fulfilled my course and the ministry, which I received of the Lord Jesus.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Until I shall have finished my course, says he, with joy.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how (clearly) these were the words not of one lamenting, but of one who forbore to make the most (of his troubles) (μετριάζοντος), of one who would instruct those (whom he addressed), and sympathize with them in the things which were befalling He says not, “I grieve indeed, but one must needs bear it:” “but,” says he, “of none of those things do I make account, neither do I have,” i.e. account “my life dear to me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why this again? not to extol himself, but to teach them, as by the former words, humility, so by these, fortitude and boldness: “I have it not precious,” i.e. “I love it not before this: I account it more precious to finish my course, to testify.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And he says not, “to preach,” “to teach”—but what says he? “to testify (διαμαρτύρασθαι)—the Gospel of the grace of God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He is about to say something more uncomfortable (φορτικώτερον), namely, “I am pure from the blood of all men (because on my part) there is nothing lacking:” he is about to lay upon them the whole weight and burden: so he first mollifies their feelings by saying, “And now behold I know that ye shall see my face no more.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The consolation is twofold: both that “my face ye shall see no more,” for in heart I am with you: and that it was not they alone (who should see him no more): for, “ye shall see my face no more, ye all, among whom I have gone about preaching the Kingdom.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that he may well (say), "Wherefore I take you to record (read διὸ μαρτ. for διαμαρτ.),—seeing I shall be with you no more—“that I am pure from the blood of all men.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark how he terrifies them, and troubled and afflicted as their souls are, how hard he rubs them (ἐπιτρίβει)?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But it was necessary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I have not shunned,” he says, “to declare unto you all the counsel of God.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, he who does not speak, has blood to answer for: that is, murder!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing could be more terrifying than this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He shows that they also, if they do it not, have blood to answer for.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So, whereas he seems to be justifying himself, in fact he is terrifying them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Take heed therefore unto yourselves, and to all the flock, over the which the Holy Ghost hath made you overseers (or, bishops) to feed the Church of God (see note 3), which He hath purchased with His own blood.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Do you mark? he enjoins them two things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Neither success in bringing others right of itself is any gain—for, I fear, he says, “lest by any means, when I have preached to others, I myself should be a cast-away” (1 Cor. ix.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27); nor the being diligent for one’s self alone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For such an one is selfish, and seeks his own good only, and is like to him who buried his talent.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Take heed to yourselves:” this he says, not because our own salvation is more precious than that of the flock, but because, when we take heed to ourselves, then the flock also is a gainer.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In which the Holy Ghost hath made you overseers, to feed the Church of God.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, it is from the Spirit ye have your ordination.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is one constraint: then he says, “To feed the Church of the Lord.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! another obligation: the Church is the Lord’s.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And a third: “which He hath purchased with His own blood.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It shows how precious the concern is; that the peril is about no small matters, seeing that even His own blood He spared not.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He indeed, that he might reconcile those who were enemies, poured out even His blood: but thou, even when they are become thy friends, art not able to retain them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I know this, that after my departing shall grievous wolves enter in among you, not sparing the flock.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again he engages (ἐπιστρέφει) them from another quarter, from the things which should come after: as when he says, “We wrestle not against flesh and blood.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After my departing,” he says, “grievous wolves shall enter in among you” (Eph. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 12); twofold the evil, both that he himself would not be present, and that others would assail them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Then why depart, if thou knowest this beforehand?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Spirit draws me, he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both “wolves,” and “grievous, not sparing the flock;” and what is worse, even “from among your own selves:” the grievous thing (this), when the war is moreover an intestine war.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The matter is exceeding serious, for it is “the Church of the Lord:” great the peril for with blood He redeemed it: mighty the war, and twofold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Also of your own selves shall men arise, speaking perverse things, to draw away disciples after them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “How then? what comfort shall there be?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Therefore watch, and remember, that by the space of three years I ceased not to warn every one night and day with tears.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how many strong expressions are here: “with tears,” and “night and day,” and “every one.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it was not that if he saw many, then he came in (to the work), but even were it for a single soul, he was capable of doing everything (for that one soul).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So it was, in fact, that he compacted them together (συνεκρότησεν) (so firmly as he did).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Enough done on my part: three years have I remained:” they had establishing enough, he says; enough of roofing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With tears,” he says.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou that the tears were on this account?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The bad man grieves not: grieve thou: perhaps he will grieve also.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As, when the sick man sees his physician partaking of food, he also is incited to do the same: so likewise here, when he sees thee weeping, he is softened: he will be a good and great man.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Not knowing,” he says, “the things that shall befall me.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then is this why thou departest?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no means; on the contrary (I know that), “bonds and afflictions await me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That (there are) trials, I know, but of what kind I know not: which was more grievous.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But none of these things move me” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 24): for do not suppose that I say these things as lamenting them: for “I hold not my own life dear.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is to raise up their minds that he says all this, and to persuade them not only not to flee, but also to bear nobly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore it is that he calls it a “course” and a “ministry,” on the one hand, showing it to be glorious from its being a race, on the other, showing what was due from it, as being a ministry.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I am a minister: nothing more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Having comforted them, that they might not grieve that he was so evil entreated, and having told them that he endured those things “with joy,” and having shown the fruits of them, then (and not before) he brings in that which would give them pain, that he may not overwhelm their minds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And now behold,” etc. “Wherefore I take you to record, that I am pure from the blood of all men, because I have not shrunk from declaring unto you the whole counsel of God” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 25–27): * * * that (counsel) which concerns the present matter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For I know this,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “What then,” someone might say, “thinkest thou thyself so great? if thou shouldest depart, are we to die?”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I say not this, he replies, that my absence causeth this: but what?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That there should rise up against you certain of another sort: he says not, “because of my departing,” but “after my departing:” that is, after his going on his journey.—And yet this thing has happened already: much more (then will it happen) hereafter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then we have the cause, “to draw away disciples after them.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That there are heresies, this is the cause, and no other than this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then comes also consolation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if He “purchased” it “with His own blood,” He will assuredly stand forward in its defence.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Night and day,” he says, “I cease not to warn with tears.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This might well be said in our case also: and though the speech seems to refer peculiarly to the teachers, it is common also to the disciples.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what, though I speak and exhort and weep night and day, while the disciple obeys not?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore it is that he says, “I take you to record:” since also himself says, “I am pure from the blood of all men: for I have not shunned to declare unto you.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, this only is to be a teacher, to declare, to preach, to instruct, shrink from nothing, to exhort night and day: but if, while one is doing all this, nothing comes of it, ye know what remains.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then ye have another justification: “I am pure from the blood of all men.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Think not that these words are spoken to us only: for indeed this speech is addressed to you also, that ye should attend to the things spoken, that ye should not start away from the hearing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What can I do?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! each day I rend myself with crying out, “Depart from the theatres:” and many laugh at us: “Desist from swearing, from covetousness:” numberless are our exhortations, and there is none to hear us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But I do not discourse during night?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Fain would I do this also in the night time, and at your tables, if it were possible that one could be divided into ten thousand pieces, so as to be present with you and discourse.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if once in the week we call to you, and ye shrink back, and some of you do not even come here, and you that do come, depart having received no profit,—what shall we do?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Many I know even sneer at us, that we are forever discoursing about the same things: so wearisome are we become to you by very satiety.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But for this not we are to blame, but the hearers may thank themselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he indeed who is making good progress, rejoices to hear the same things always; it seems to be his praises that he hears spoken: but he who does not wish to get on, seems even to be annoyed, and though he hear the same thing but twice, it seems to him that he is hearing it often.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “I am pure,” he says, “from the blood of all men.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This was fit and proper for Paul to say, but we dare not say it, conscious as we are of numberless faults.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore for him the ever vigilant, ever at hand, the man enduring all things for the sake of the salvation of his disciples, it was fit and proper to say this: but we must say that of Moses, “The Lord was wroth with me for your sakes” (Deut. iii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 26), because ye lead us also into many sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For when we are dispirited at seeing you make no progress, is not the greater part of our strength struck down?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what, I ask you has been done?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Lo! by the grace of God we also have now passed the space of three years, not indeed night and day exhorting you, but doing this, often every third day, or every seventh.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What more has come of it?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We accuse, we rebuke, we weep, we are in anguish, although not openly, yet in heart.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But those (inward) tears are far more bitter than these (outward ones): for these indeed bring a kind of relief to the feelings of the sorrowful, whereas those aggravate it, and bind it fast.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Since when there is any cause of grief, and one cannot give vent to the sorrow, lest he should seem to be vainglorious, think what he suffers!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Were it not that people would tax me with excessive love of display, you would see me each day shedding fountains of tears: but to those my chamber is witness, and my hours of solitude.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For believe me I have (at times) despaired of my own salvation, but from my mourning on your account, I have not even leisure to bemoan my own evils: so entirely are ye all in all to me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And whether I perceive you to be advancing, then, for very delight, I am not sensible of my own evils: or whether I see you not advancing, such is my grief, I again dismiss my own cares from my thoughts: brightening up on account of your good things, though I myself have evils without number, and saddened on account of your painful things, though my own successes are without number.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what hope is there for the teacher, when his flock is destroyed?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What kind of life, what kind of expectation is there for him?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: With what sort of confidence will he stand up before God? what will he say?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For grant that he has nothing laid to his charge, has no punishment to suffer, but is “pure from the blood of all men:” yet even so will he suffer a grief incurable: since fathers also though they be not liable to be called to account for their children’s sins, nevertheless have grief and vexation.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And this profits them nothing, nor shields them (προίσταται).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “For it is they that watch for our souls, as those that must give account.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This seems to be a fearful thing: to me this gives no concern after your destruction.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For whether I give account, or not, it is no profit to me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Might it be, that ye were saved, and I to give account because of you: ye saved, and I charged with not having fulfiled my part!

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For my anxiety is not that you should be saved through me as the means, but only that you should be saved, no matter by what person as the instrument.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Ye know not the pangs of spiritual childbirth, how overpowering they are; how he who is in travail with this birth, would rather be cut into ten thousand pieces, than see one of those to whom he has given birth perishing and undone.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Whence shall we persuade you?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: By no other argument indeed, but by what has been done, in all that regards you we shall clear ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We too shall be able to say, that in nothing have we “shrunk from declaring” to you the whole truth: nevertheless we grieve: and that we do grieve, is manifest from the numberless plans we lay and contrivances we devise.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And yet we might say to ourselves, What matters it to me?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I have done my part, “I am pure from” (their) “blood:” but this is not enough for comfort.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If we could tear open our heart, and show it to you, ye would see with what largeness it holds (you) within it, both women and children and men; for such is the power of love, that it makes the soul more spacious than the heaven.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Receive us,” says (Paul): “we have wronged no man, ye are not straitened in us.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (2 Cor. vii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He had all Corinth in his heart, and says, “Ye are not straitened: be ye also enlarged” (2 Cor. vi.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13); but I myself could not say this, for I well know, that ye both love me and receive me.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But what is the profit either from my love or from yours, when the things pertaining to God thrive not in us?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a ground for greater sorrow, an occasion of worse mischief (λύμης, al. λύπης).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: I have nothing to lay to your charge: “for I bear you record, that, if it had been possible, ye would have plucked out your own eyes, and have given them to me.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “We yearn not only to give you the Gospel, but also our own souls.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (1 Thess. ii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We are loved and we love (you): but this is not the question.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But let us love Christ, “for the first commandment is, Thou shalt love the Lord thy God: and the second is like unto it, And thy neighbor as thyself.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Matt. xxii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We have the second, we need the first: need the first, exceedingly, both I and you.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: We have it, but not as we ought.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us love Him: ye know how great a reward is laid up for them that love Christ: let us love Him with fervor of soul, that, enjoying his goodwill, we may escape the stormy waves of this present life, and be found worthy to obtain the good things promised to them that love Him, through the grace and mercy of His only-begotten Son, with whom to the Father, together with the Holy Ghost, be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
